sources.full: SW5e: Black Cipher - A SW5e D&D One-Shot

trait.name: Bipolarity Shielding
entries: The Stormtrooper is shrouded in a Red Kinetic Polarity Field. He has resistance to all but psychic damage unless the attack is 'Attuned' to the opposite color (blue). Conversely, he is vulnerable to 'Blue Attuned' damage.

trait.name: Terrible Shot
entries: The stormtrooper has permanent disadvantage when making a blaster attack.

trait.name: Bipolarity Shielding
entries: The Stormtrooper is shrouded in a Blue Kinetic Polarity Field. He has resistance to all but psychic damage unless the attack is 'Attuned' to the opposite color (red). Conversely, he is vulnerable to 'Red Attuned' damage.

trait.name: Terrible Shot
entries: The stormtrooper has permanent disadvantage when making a blaster attack.

trait.name: Bipolarity Shielding
entries: The Stormtrooper is shrouded in a Red Kinetic Polarity Field. He has resistance to all but psychic damage unless the attack is 'Attuned' to the opposite color (blue). Conversely, he is vulnerable to 'Blue Attuned' damage.

trait.name: Terrible Shot
entries: The stormtrooper has permanent disadvantage when making a blaster attack.

trait.name: Bipolarity Shielding
entries: The Stormtrooper is shrouded in a Blue Kinetic Polarity Field. He has resistance to all but psychic damage unless the attack is 'Attuned' to the opposite color (red). Conversely, he is vulnerable to 'Red Attuned' damage.

trait.name: Terrible Shot
entries: The stormtrooper has permanent disadvantage when making a blaster attack.

reaction.name: Experimental Phase Shift (2/Day)
entries: As a reaction when he would otherwise take damage, the trooper can phase out of reality until the start of their next turn.
When invoking this mod, roll a {@dice 1d6} -- on a {@b 1}, the experimental mod instead shorts out and inflicts {@b 30 ion damage} to the trooper.
If successfully phase-shifted, the trooper becomes immune to all damage except psychic, ion*, and critical hits**, and can move through solid objects (including walls).
*Ion damage has a 1 in 6 ({@dice 1d6}) chance of disrupting the phase field and causing it to fail.
**Critical hits automatically disrupt the phase field.

trait.name: Bipolarity Shielding
entries: Tremayne is shrouded in a Kinetic Polarity Field that swaps between Red and Blue every time he is hit by an attack. While the field is active, he has resistance to all damage unless the attack is 'Attuned' to the opposite color, in which case he is vulnerable to that damage.

reaction.name: Experimental Phase-Shift
entries: As a reaction when he would otherwise take damage, Tremayne can phase out of reality until the start of his next turn.
When invoking this mod, roll a {@dice 1d6} -- on a {@b 1}, the experimental mod instead shorts out and inflicts {@b 30 ion damage} to Tremayne.
If successfully phase-shifted, Tremayne becomes immune to all damage except psychic, ion*, and critical hits**, and can move through solid objects (including walls).
*Ion damage has a 1 in 6 ({@dice 1d6}) chance of disrupting the phase field and causing it to fail.
**Critical hits automatically disrupt the phase field.

item.name: Stimpack
entries: A stimpack is a single-use auto-injector loaded with concentrated kolto. As an action, you can use this stimpack on yourself or one creature you can touch. The creature regains {@dice 4d4 + 4} hit points (equivalent to a {@i potion of greater healing} in the core rules). The stimpack is expended when used. It has no effect on droids or constructs.

item.name: Kyber Resonance Shard
entries: This crystalline kyber shard seats in a lightsaber emitter stack, gas vent, power-core housing, or similar cavity. You can install it into a single weapon you touch—including any lightsaber, lightfoil, vibroweapon, or manufactured ranged weapon.

Once installed, that weapon is treated as an enhanced weapon: while you wield it, you have a +1 bonus to attack and damage rolls with it. A weapon can host only one such shard at a time; installing another destroys the previous one.

item.name: Adrenaline Shot
entries: This autoinjector delivers a hyperadrenal stimulant. As a bonus action, you inject yourself, restoring up to 3 class resources (e.g. Rage, Bardic Inspiration, Ki points, etc.) at the cost of one of your unspent Hit Dice each. The shot is consumed once it is used.

data.name: Running this Adventure
entries: This adventure is designed to be run as a one-shot adventure. It is not intended to be run as a campaign. The adventure is designed to be run with a group of 5 players at level 5, but can be run with as few as 3 players if desired.
Before the adventure begins, show this Opening Crawl:

data.name: Cold Open
entries: {@cue [Load Image #2: HoloNet Chamber]|media}
Read the following aloud to set the tone for the antagonist and the high-level stakes.

The HoloNet chamber is sterile and dark, illuminated only by the rhythmic blue flicker of a massive holoprojection. A slender, caped man with a glowing red cybernetic eye, kneels on the metallic floor, his head bowed in deference.
A towering, ominous figure stares back out of the transmission, his deep, raspy, mechanical breathing echoing through the silent room.
{@i [Darth Vader breathing sounds]}

{@b Tremayne:} {@i [High Coruscant Accent]} "The phase lattice is locked, My Lord," the kneeling man says, his voice steady but cautious. "Black Cipher remains stable across preliminary trials—the stacks hold cleanly under live combat stress. Our harnessed infantry are effectively impervious; volleys simply wash across their polarity shells. Rebels will exhaust themselves engaging targets they cannot hurt."

{@b Darth Vader:} "See that it remains so, Inquisitor Tremayne," the armored titan coldly replies. "The Emperor's patience with your 'intellectual approach' is nearing its end. If the phase project slips, your life is forfeit. There will be no further warnings."

The hologram vanishes into a single point of light. The man stands, adjusting the sleeve over his mechanical limb as he looks toward the door. "Commander, re-double your effforts to harden phase-shift redundancies NOW. We can't afford The Black Cipher to have a single exploitable weakness. What are you waiting for?! GET TO WORK!"
The Inquisitor stands, straightening his posture. He brushes his cape back over his shoulders, and exits the chamber.

data.name: The Briefing
entries: {@cue [Load Image #3: Mission Briefing]|media}
Read the following aloud:

You stand in a sparse white conference room aboard the Nebulon-B frigate, The Ressurgence, amoung Jedi, rebel soldiers, and pilots. General Crix Madine stands by the viewport, his hands clasped behind his back as he watches the stars streak by in hyperspace. He turns as you enter.

{@b Madine:} "Gentlemen, we're running out of time. The Empire is pouring their resources into an experimental new technology, codenamed Black Cipher. It is a firmware update to all networked military outposts to upgrade their stormtrooper armor with polarity shielding. A polarity-empowered shield is impervious to all known weaponry. If their system-wide update is successful, they'll expand the scope to encrypt whole ships with this shielding technology, and eventually entire armadas. We have to destroy this technology in it's cradle before it's too late. If we can't, our upcoming offensive will be a slaughter."
He gestures to a young, thin man obsessively cleaning a data-tablet.
{@b Madine:} "This is Caspar Vex. He's a... specialist in Imperial logic-gates." Caspar looks up, eyes darting over your gear with a scoff.

{@b Madine:} "Our intel has located the source facility. You will be dropping into the {@b Seswenna Sector}, specifically the {@b Orelia System}. Your target is the forest moon of {@b Vora-7}. Vex is essential. You will land ten clicks from the {@b Sensor Array Node}. Once that post is blind, you'll escort him into the heart of the {@b Black Cipher Mainframe}."

{@b Caspar:} {@i [Nerdy Voice]} "Specialist? General, please. I'm the only person in this quadrant who can even read the source code for Black Cipher. And you've paired me with... this? A few lightsabers and some muscle? This is a job for a scalpel, not a pack of stooges with blasters."

"Intel suggests the facility is overseen by High Inquisitor Tremayne. I knew him in the Imperial Academy. He isn't a brute like Vader; he's a philosopher of war. He'll predict your moves before you make them. Don't give him the chance."
"The shuttle is prepped in Bay 4. Good luck, and may the Force be with you."

entries.name: Approach
entries: {@cue [Load Image #4: Shuttle Landing]|media}
As the adventurers descend into the atmosphere of the forest moon in their transport, read the following aloud:

The stealth transport shuttle settles into a mossy clearing. As the ramp drops, the fresh smell of wet bark fills your nostrils. You are 2 clicks south of the first target: the Sensor Array Node; the forest canopy is dense enough to hide your approach from overhead patrols, but it means the rest of the journey is on foot.
Suddenly, your comlinks crackle with a secure burst.

{@b Madine (Radio):} "Team, you're on the ground. Remember the priority: disable the motion sensors and long-range uplink at that node first. If you don't blind them now, the Mainframe will see you coming from a sector away. Get Vex to that terminal."
{@b Caspar:} "Yes, do try not to give away our position before we get to the first objective, goons."
As the players trudge through the forest towards the Sensor Array Node, read the following aloud:

The hike takes you through a landscape of ancient, moss-slicked roots and massive trunks that block out much of the natural light. Until, finally, you see the {@b Sensor Array Node} ahead of you through the trees—a squat bunker, burried into the forrest ground.
{@cue [Load Image #5: Sensor Array Node]|media}
Four troopers stand guard. They are dressed in white plastoid armor... but they seem to be enveloped in auras of energy. Two are red, two are blue.
{@b Caspar:} "Oh, stupendous," Caspar hisses from behind a tree. "Those looks like some kind of Kinetic Polarity Fields. Ha! Good luck with this one, dumb jocks."

entries.name: Sensor Array Encounter
Red/Blue Shields
entries: Troopers have Resistance to all damage except for psychic and mental-based or sensory-based Force powers.

entries.name: The Mechanic
items: Striking a trooper's colored shield 'attunes' a PC's weapon to that color energy. A single weapon can be attuned to only one color at a time.
Attuned weapons ignore the Resistance of the opposite colored shield. Red-charged weapons damage blue enemies and vice versa.
Blaster or projectile fire deflects off of a shield, charged with that color energy, and can be carefully ricocheted off of the charged shield to hit a different enemy.
If two oppositely colored troopers collide, they both take 2d6 ion damage and are knocked back 1 square.

entries.name: Sensor Array Node Encounter
notes: {@i If the players haven't figured out the color-based mechanics, read the following aloud:}

{@b Caspar:} "Look, if you nitwits can actually coordinate for once: It's a simple circuit-break. Even a Wookiee could understand it. Do you hear what I'm saying? HIT BLUE WITH RED ENERGY, AND HIT THE RED WITH BLUE ENERGY? Sheesh."

entries: Once the guards have been defeated, read the following aloud:
Caspar inserts his data-spike into the terminal and begins to slice his way through the emperial firewalls.
Present the first puzzle for the players to solve. Completing it represents Caspar successfully disabling the motion sensors and long-range comms.
{@cue [Load Image #6: Sensor Array Puzzle]|media}
{@i Correct Answer: THATS NO MOON}
Once the players have solved the puzzle, read the following aloud:

"Ha! We're in, dummies! Now, to decompile the ternary logic-tree and reticulate the sensor node protocols aaand... Voila! Motion sensors and long-range comms have been silently disabled. A-you're welcome."

data.name: Act 2: Speeder Bike Chase

entries: Caspar pulls his data-spike from the terminal with a smug click just as a crackle of static erupts from the building's external PA system. "Comms are dead! They've scrambled the long-range uplink!" one panicked voice shouts. A second voice snaps back: "Check the emergency frequency! If the signal won't go, we have to warn the {@b Black Cipher Mainframe} in person!"

There's a brief fumbling sound before the first voice screams, "WAIT. BARNES?? ARE YOU LEANING ON THE INTERCOM SWITCH?!" Barnes' voice yelps, "DANK FARRIK, CAN THEY HEAR US?!" followed by a sharp squelch as the speakers abruptly go dead. Then, from somewhere inside the bunker, you hear a muffled crash as some kind of equipment is clumsily knocked over.
{@cue Let players investigate the voices, time permitting.}
As you move to intercept, suddenly, a scout trooper bursts from the main blast door, nearly running into you. He yelps, turns, and sprints toward the back side of the building.
{@cue If anyone wants to try to stop him, roll an initiative "check" to see who gets the honor}.
He doesn't even flinch. Your blast/weapon passes right through the man. Hold on, you KNOW you hit center mass... Is it a hologram?

Before you can register the confusion, the high-pitched whine of 74-Z engines erupts from behind the bunker. You round the corner just in time to see the two speeders already in motion; as they gathers speed, their solid frames ripple and wash into a translucent ghostlike forms. Beside them, a third bike and its ghostly rider shimmer into translucency. Driving straight toward an ancient Iron-Oak in their path, they do not swerve; instead, they accelerate straight through the massive trunk like smoke through a screen, vanishing into the deep forest.
Caspar gasps. "Incredible! Phased-shifted! That kind of tech is only theoretical!"
Madine's voice crackles over the radio:
{@b Madine:} "What's happening? Our sensors show 3 Imperial speeders heading for the Mainframe. You must stop them, or they will raise the alarm. Move NOW!"
{@cue [Load Image #7: Speeder Chase]|media}

entries.name: Speeder Chase Skill Challenge
entries: This encounter is a theater-of-the-mind skill challenge. Give each rider a {@b d8} with the current face showing distance from the scouts: {@b 8} ({@i far}) down to {@b 0} ({@i interception}).

entries.name: Countdown & Scale
entries: {@b Garrison countdown:} Run the pursuit across {@b 10 numbered rounds}; if {@b round 10} ends and neither rider has been stopped, they punch through to allied lines ({@i see The Finish Line}). Call the round number aloud.
{@b Scale:} Each {@b distance} increment is roughly {@b 20 ft} of forest gap for narration; the number on the d8 still sets attack and save modifiers from the {@b Distance Modifiers} table.

entries.name: The Distance Pool
entries: All players start at {@b Distance 8}. The objective is to eliminate or disable the scouts, or reach {@b Distance 0} to intercept the targets, before they reach the {@b Black Cipher Mainframe}.

entries.name: Round Sequence
entries: Each round, a player chooses {@b one} of the following options on their turn:

items: {@b 1. Gain Ground:} Roll a {@skill Piloting} check. On a {@b 10+}, reduce your distance by 1. On a {@b 20+}, reduce your distance by 2.
{@b 2. Take Action:} Use an action (attack, power, etc.). Effectiveness is dictated by your current distance pool.

entries.caption: Distance Modifiers
rows: Auto-hit / auto-success on relevant hostile actions ({@i no roll})

entries.name: Obstacles
entries: {@b When obstacles fire:} Roll {@dice 1d6} at the start of each round before PCs act. On {@b 1–3}, expose every rider still in the chase to a hazard this round; on {@b 4–6}, the lane stays clear enough to ignore ({@i still roughly 50%; about one obstacle every two rounds on average}). For a fixed cadence, skip the die and trigger hazards only on {@b even-numbered rounds} (2, 4, 6, 8, 10).
Each affected rider resolves the hazard immediately, before Gain Ground/Take Action, by choosing:

items: {@b Drop Back:} Increase your distance by 1 to bleed speed and glide around the snag safely.

{@b Push Through:} Hold the line: roll {@skill Piloting} {@b DC 5}. Failure wrecks your bike and takes you out of the chase. After resolving that check, roll {@dice d20}; if the result is {@b 1}, debris shears a repulsor and the bike goes down anyway ({@i ≈ 5% chance per Push Through attempt}). Treat {@i 2–20 on the crash die as no extra effect beyond Piloting result.}

entries.name: Attacks on the Scouts
entries: Use each rider's statblock for regular attacks and saves—reaching {@b 0 Hit Points} drops that scout from the chase. Reward player creativity ({@i Force-pushing a rider into an obstacle, creating illusory terrain to confuse, etc.}).

Each scout has one remaining use of their phase shield as a reaction. It lasts until the start of their next turn. The next time they try to use it, it glitches and dies, leaving them vulnerable to attack.

Play for {@i impact and creativity}; stretching to round 10 is the pressure valve, not a goal. Aim for {@b everyone} to snag at least one meaningful moment before bikes start dropping—then let the climax land hard.

entries.name: The Finish Line
entries: The chase succeeds when {@b all three} scouts are dumped—each requires a cinematic takedown from whichever PC pins that bike—or after an adventurer reaches {@b Distance 0} and "intercepts" the enemy. If {@b round 10} concludes with either rider mobile, the chase ends in failure, and the sector is alerted. Shorten or extend the countdown if pacing demands it.
{@cue [Load Image #8: Mainframe in the Distance]|media}
{@i A: If the chase ends in failure, read the following aloud:}

The scream of the speeder engines fades into the distance as the scouts break through the final line of Iron-Oaks. A moment later, a searing red signal flare arcs high above the canopy, bursting into a jagged Imperial sigil that hangs mockingly in the blue mist.

The silence of the forest is shattered by the low, rhythmic thrum of base-wide klaxons. From the direction of the {@b Black Cipher Mainframe}, you hear the heavy, synchronized double-thump of AT-ST walkers pivoting to intercept and the distant barking of squad leaders rallying their troops.
Your comlink snaps to priority band.

{@b Madine (Radio):} "Well, team, the garrison knows you're coming, now. But the Mainframe stays the objective. We'll have to commit strike squadrons for air support to draw the base's heavy defenses away: AT-AT's, ion cannons, and turbolasers. That noise will be your cover, now. Don't waste it."
Even as he speaks, the forest fills with distant laserfire, drawing the heavy artilery toward Rebel blips more than toward you.

{@b Caspar:} "Oh, bravo! Just magnificent! Why settle for a quiet infiltration when we can invite the entire Imperial garrison to a shootout? The recursive buffers are locking down as we speak! If we don't reach that mainframe in the next five minutes, we might as well march ourselves into a holding cell!"
Mounting your speeder bikes, you race towards the Black Cipher Mainframe, time no longer on your side.
{@i B: If the chase ends in success, read the following aloud:}
The scream of your speeder engines fades as your party regroups, the scouts finally eliminated.
Madine's voice crackles over the radio:

{@b Madine (Radio):} "Well done, team. You've secured the first objective and prevented the Imperial troops from sounding the alarm. Now, make your way towards the Mainframe, get Vex access to plant his logic bomb virus, and then get out of there before the Empire realizes what's happened."
{@b Caspar:} "Well! I'm impressed you were able to stop the scouts after all. I guess even a blind womprat catches a scurrier every now and then, am I right?"
Mounting your speeder bikes, you continue carefully towards the Black Cipher Mainframe, unseen and unexpected.

data.name: Act 3: The Mainframe Infiltration
entries: Read the following aloud:
{@cue [Load Image #9: Energy Sensor]|media}

The Mainframe building looms in the distance, a towering structure of metal and glass. Its cluttered roofline prickles with gantries, some with ladders and scaffolding, and atop the central annex pad,the dorsal plating and neatly folded wings of a {@b Lambda-class T-4a shuttle}. At ground level, patrols amble by in regular patterns, alert and scanning the horizons. You bide your time, waiting for a lull in the manpower guarding the door, and eventually you spot a simple pair of Stormtroopers heading towards the blast door... this is as good as it's gonna get.

{@b Caspar:} "Wait!" Caspar hisses. "Do you not see that energy sensor?!" He points to a spherical object mounted sturdily to the metal wall. "It's designed to detect emmission of any weapon-based energy signatures. If you fire a blaster or charge out swinging your little glow sticks, you'll trigger it and alert them to our presence."
{@b Madine:} "I'm afraid he's right. You're going to have to take care of this guard without weapons."
{@cue Give the players a chance to try to confuse or eliminate the guard without weapons.}
You approach the blast door, and Caspar inserts his data-spike into the terminal.

entries.name: The Entrance Puzzle
entries: The blast door is sealed with a logic-scramble.
{@cue [Load Image #10: Mainframe Entrance Puzzle]|media}
Caspar sighs, hovering his fingers over the terminal. "Arranging these linguistic shards should bypass the lock. I'd do it, but I'm busy babysitting phase-drift spikes in the coherence logs."
{@i Correct Answer: LET THE WOOKIE WIN}

entries.name: The Corridor Crawl
entries: Once the puzzle is solved, read aloud:
The terminal glows blue, and the blast door slides open.
{@cue [Load Image #11: Mainframe Corridors]|media}
The interior is a freezing labyrinth of white panels and humming servers. Occasional MSE-6 mouse droids zip across the floor, chirping with angst.
{@cue Let the players explore at their own pace, and allow them to take out a few mouse droids.}

entries.name: Supply Closet
entries: Cracking the door lock on the Supply Closet requires solving a puzzle:
{@cue [Load Image #19: Supply Closet Puzzle]|media}
{@i Correct Answer: DO OR DO NOT}
Players who investigate the supply closet find:

items: {@dice 1d4 + 1} {@item Stimpack|sw5eOrcnogBlackCipher|stimpacks}
Five {@item Adrenaline Shot|sw5eOrcnogBlackCipher|adrenaline shots}

entries.name: Weapons Cache
entries: Cracking the door lock on the Weapons Cache requires solving a puzzle:
{@cue [Load Image #20: Weapons Cache Puzzle]|media}
{@i Correct Answer: YOURE MY ONLY HOPE}
Players who investigate the weapons cache find a few items of interest:

items: Five {@item Kyber Resonance Shard|sw5eOrcnogBlackCipher|kyber resonance shards} (turn any weapon into a +1)
Eight E-11 Blaster Rifles (statistically equivalent to a shortbow in range and damage)
{@dice 1d6} {@item Grenade, Ion|sw5ephb|Ion Grenades} (30 ft range, 10 ft. radius, DC 16 DEX save, {@dice 2d6} ion damage / half on a save)

entries: Once the players round the ambush setup corner, read the following aloud:
You round a corner and see a lone Stormtrooper. He freezes, eyeing your entire armed team, lets out a high-pitched 'Nope!', and bolts down the hallway.
The fleeing stormtrooper is leading any pursuers into a trap. A platoon of Stormtroopers is waiting around the corner, their blasters trained on the entrance to the corridor.
{@cue Any player who plays into the famous Star Wars scene recreation (whether intentionally or otherwise), instantly receives Heroic Inspiration!}

entries.name: Corridor Encounter
notes: {@i Tactics:} troopers funnel fire down the choke point; tuck server racks in for {@b half cover} if you want the fight to linger.
{@i Pacing:} if the hallway feels overstuffed with bodies, stagger reinforcements ({@i half the troopers sprint in on round 2}).

entries.name: The Nexus Chamber Door
entries: The final door to the core is labeled: {@b The Nexus Chamber}.
Your comlink hums one last time.
{@b Madine (Radio):} "That's it. Energy readings in that room are off the chart. Secure that mainframe or the Empire wins. Good luck, and may the Force be with you."
{@b Caspar:} "Yes, yes, very inspiring. Now, move your 'Force' out of the way and let Technology {@i actually} solve the problem. Just one last security protocol to override..."

entries.name: The Nexus Chamber Door Puzzle
entries: The Nexus Chamber Door is sealed with a logic-scramble.
{@cue [Load Image #12: Nexus Chamber Door Puzzle]|media}
{@i Correct Answer: NEVER TELL ME THE ODDS}

entries.name: The Nexus Chamber
The Final Ambush Encounter
entries: {@cue [Load Image #13: The Nexus Chamber]|media}

The blast doors hiss open, venting a cloud of sub-zero coolant fog into the hallway. Beyond lies the Nexus Chamber: a cathedral of data where rings of server racks spiral upward into a vaulted gloom. In the center, the Black Cipher core—a rotating pillar of blinding blue data—hums with the power of the entire sector.
High Inquisitor Tremayne stands before the core, hands laced politely behind his back. His form is translucent, shimmering like a ghost in the machine. He doesn't even turn to look at you.

{@b Tremayne:} "How predictable. I'm just curious... Did you really think we wouldn't notice your approach? You've walked straight into the trap I've been tending." He finally turns slowly to face you, "But no fanfare necessary—you delivered {@i yourselves}."

He raises a hand. Above you, the sound of boots ringing on metal catwalks echoes as a squad of stormtroopers takes position in a horseshoe around you, E-11 barrels dipping from the railings, their sights stitching a web of red light across the floor. You are surrounded, from an elevated position.
{@cue [Load Image #14: The Nexus Chamber]|media}
"{@b Tremayne:} It's over, Jedi.  I have the high-ground."

{@b Caspar:} "Smoke this! I'm patching in!" Caspar dives between two reinforced server racks, slamming his data-spike into a maintenance port. Blaster fire begins to ping off the metal. "I need some time! If I can't plant the virus, the Rebellion is finished! Just keep those blasters off me!"

entries.name: Phase 1: The Siege (Rounds 1-4)
entries: During this phase, Inquisitor Tremayne is {@b Static Phased}. He is completely out of sync with reality and cannot be targeted. Due to his legendary Force-attunement and cold logic, he is {@b immune to psychic damage, illusions, and all mind-altering effects}.
The players must defend Caspar from the catwalk troopers. While Caspar has cover from the racks, he is vulnerable to specific hazards.

entries.name: The Ambush Mechanics
items: Troopers fire from the catwalks, which provide them {@b Three-Quarters Cover}.
1/3 of the Troopers have {@b Blue Shields} (Resistance to all but Blue/Kinetic damage).
1/3 of the Troopers have {@b Red Shields} (Resistance to all but Red/Kinetic damage).
1/3 of the Troopers can use a reaction to {@b Phase Shift} for 1 round when targeted.
{@b Starting in Round 2}, two {@creature Droideka|sw5eOrcnogRugosa|droidekas} roll in from the back of the room to join the fight. They activate their shields: one red, one blue.

entries.name: Threats to Caspar
entries: At the start of the Troopers' turn, roll 1d6 on the {@b Nexus Hazard Table} to determine the threat to the slicer.

entries.caption: Nexus Hazard Table
rows: {@b Blaster Graze:} Blasts ricochet off the racks. Caspar takes 1 HP damage. A PC can use a reaction to physically shield him, taking the hit instead.
{@b Polarity Grenade:} A trooper lobs a Red or Blue grenade into the alcove. A PC must use an action to throw it back (DC 12 Athletics). If failed, Caspar takes 2d6 damage of that color.
{@b Rappel Ambush:} Two troopers drop down on cables onto the ground level, attempting to disrupt his work with vibro-bayonets.

entries.name: Phase 2: The Sacrifice (Round 4)
entries: At the end of Round 4, Tremayne grows bored of the stalemate.
{@b Caspar:} "Almost got it...."
Then, without warning, a streak of crimson light flashes through the room and a lightsaber impales Caspar through the chest. The slicer's eyes go wide. The blaster fire halts.
Tremayne sighs. "How tedious. Even with the odds stacked in their favor, my men are so very incompetent. I prefer a more... direct intervention."
Caspar slumps against the terminal, blood slicking the keys as he looks at you. "Ready to... deploy..." he chokes out. "Just hit... the..."
His head falls forward, dead. The terminal screen flickers with a final prompt.
To execute the logic-bomb, players must solve the final quote scramble:
{@cue [Load Image #15: Final Puzzle]|media}
{@i Correct Answer: ITS A TRAP!}

entries.name: Phase 3: The Destabilized Core
entries: Once the puzzle is solved, the Black Cipher core begins to melt down.

The terminal flashes a brilliant green. A high-pitched, harmonic whine begins to build within the blue pillar, and the entire floor begins to shudder. Seeing the facility go critical, any troopers remaining break formation and bolt for the exits.
Tremayne's form suddenly solidifies as his phase-module burns out with a puff of ozone. He ignites his blade and snaps a heavy {@b Red Shield} into place, his eyes burning with aristocratic hatred.
{@b Tremayne:} "You may have destroyed the Cipher... but you've merely ensured your own burial, Jedi. I will take your heads back to Lord Vader as my consolation prize!"

Electrical arcs begin to leap from server to server, as Inquisitor Tremayne's lightsaber flies back into his hand, and he drops into a Form II stance. It is just you and the Inquisitor now. "En garde!"
Now, at the start of each round, a random 10ft area is struck by lightning (DC 13 Dex save or 3d8 Lightning damage).

entries.name: Black Cipher Finale
notes: {@i "Form V? A bold choice, though your footwork is... amateur."}
{@i "That lightsaber hilt... the craftsmanship is distinctly Corellian. Such a pity it will be melted down for scrap."}

entries: Once the players defeat Inquisitor Tremayne, read the following aloud:

entries.name: The Killing Blow

entries: As the final blow lands, Tremayne staggers back, his lightfoil flickering and dying. He looks at the screaming data-core, then back at you with a mask of pure fury. Before he can speak, a massive power-conduit above him explodes, burying the Inquisitor in a rain of white-hot debris.

The room is deafening now—a cacophony of alarms and screeching metal. The floor is slick with coolant, and fire is beginning to consume the server banks. The way out is collapsing. You have to move, NOW!"
{@cue If the players do not remember the shuttle perched atop the roof, remind them...}
You spy a sign next to a set of metal stairs leading to the roof. It reads: {@b Shuttle Access}. Of course! The rooftop shuttle! You hope it's still there...
You race up the stairs to the roof, and there it is: a {@b Lambda-class T-4a shuttle}. Your ticket out of this self-destructing facility! You board and power up the engines.

entries.name: Escape!
entries: (Time-permitting) As the party flees the disintegrating base, they'll need to pass one final skill challenge to escape the moon successfully.
{@cue [Load Image #17: Escape Shuttle]|media}

entries.name: Final Skill Challenge
entries: Begin by asking the players to pick a job aboard the shuttle: Pilot, Navigator, Gunner, Tech Support, or Ready for Anything. Then, choose one skill check from the following list for each player. The party must succeed on half or more of the following skill checks to escape the moon successfully:

items:
{@b Navigator:} Radar towers, coaxium lines, and tall iron-oak trees crowd your escape vector.
{@cue DC 13 Piloting check to successfully get the shuttle airborne and safely clear of vegetation and ground structures without crashing.}

{@b Gunner:} Scattered stormtroopers notice your escape and attempt to shoot you down with blaster fire from ground-level.
{@cue DC 13 Piloting check (or Ranged weapon attack w/ proficiency vs. AC of 13) to man the aft turbolasers and take out the ground forces.}

{@b Tech Support:} Anti-air cannons pepper the sky with laser fire, and you can't tell how long before they ID you as a hostile target.
{@cue DC 13 Piloting or Technology check to hack your transponder code to ghost your ship from the Imperial automated anti-air defenses.}

{@b Pilot:} A sudden explosion from the Black Cipher Mainframe below rocks your shuttle and sends debris screaming through the air like missiles.
{@cue DC 13 Piloting check to take evasive action to avoid the debris and continue your escape.}

{@b Ready for Anything:} A rebel x-wing has locked onto you and is closing in! They don't know you're a friendly! You must act quickly to avoid being shot down by your own side!
{@cue Let the player be as creative as they like — success vs. failure is at theDM's discretion (but c'mon, it should succeed!)}

entries: If the players fail a majority of the Piloting checks, the shuttle explodes. Read the following aloud:

The shuttle explodes in a fireball, killing all on board. Flaming wreckage falls back to the moon, along with Jedi body parts, and droid parts.  Aboard the Ressurgence, Gen. Madine watches the devastation with a heavy heart.

{@b Madine:} "Sigh. Lieutenant... notify next of kin. Mission accomplished, heroes. You will be remembered for your bravery and your sacrifice on Vora-7. Tales will be told. Movies will be made! And hey, now you're all more powerful than you can possibly imagine!  May the Force be with you all in the afterlife! I guess?
If the players succeed in the skill checks, read the following aloud:
You streak through the atmosphere, the forest moon falling away as the Black Cipher Mainframe implodes into a pillar of white-hot debris. You radio General Madine with the news: mission accomplished.

The hangar bay of the rebel frigate {@i The Resurgence} swallows your transport. Before the ramp even touches the deck, the sound of hammered applause from hundreds of techs and troopers rings off the bulkheads—a wall of relief that echoes through the ship.

General Madine pushes through the crowd, his cape singed at the hem but his eyes bright. "You did it. Front-line sensors are shouting about cascading phase-shift failures—Imperial signature wash is ripping apart. You bought the Alliance time, and blood, and hope."
{@cue [Load Image #18: Victory]|media}

He grips your shoulders, unsmiling until the corner of his mouth finally betrays a grin. He turns to the crowded deck, raising a hand for one final oath: "Today you tore teeth from the tyrant's grin. Own that victory.  Today... you are Heroes of the Rebellion! May the Fourth Be With You!"
THE END

data.name: Appendix: Character Notes
entries: The following material is for Narrators only: expanded character detail, pronunciation, and setting notes.

entries.name: General Crix Madine
entries: {@i (CRICKS muh-DEEN)}

A weathered veteran of the Imperial Storm Commandos whose defection brought the Rebellion its most tactical mind, he carries the heavy burden of command in his gravelly voice and expects nothing less than absolute efficiency from those he sends into the fire.

entries.name: Voice
entries: Professional and stoic. His voice is deep and gravelly, carrying the weight of a former Imperial commander who has seen too much war.

entries.name: Caspar Vex
entries: {@i (CASS-per VEKS)}

A brilliant but insufferable Imperial logic-gate specialist with an intellect as sharp as his social skills are dull; he makes no secret of his utter disdain for the 'primitive' physical solutions of the adventurers he is forced to rely upon. Purely a liability in combat.

entries.name: Voice
entries: Incredibly nerdy, somewhat nasally, and featuring a massive, orthodontia-induced lisp that whistleth through every 'S' and 'Th' sound.

entries.name: Vora-7
entries: {@i (vor-AH SEV-en)}

A temperate forest moon in the {@b Seswenna Sector}, characterized by its ancient 'Giant Iron-Oaks' and drifting blue-gray mists. It houses the Empire's most sensitive Black Cipher prototyping—phase-shift rigs, coherence stacks, polarity emitters—all centered on the {@b Black Cipher Mainframe}.

entries.name: High Inquisitor Tremayne
entries: {@i (truh-MAIN)}

A cold and calculating Coruscanti cyborg who views the chaotic galaxy through a multi-spectrum lens of Imperial logic, preferring to dismantle his enemies with surgical precision and aristocratic disdain rather than brute force.

entries.name: Voice
entries: Calm, silky, and terrifyingly polite. He speaks with an aristocratic precision, never raising his voice, treating combat like a lecture in a classroom.

entries.name: Battle Tactics
entries: Tremayne is known for letting his opponents draw first. In the final encounter, he should stay perfectly still, arms behind his back, while his troopers engage. He only ignites his blade when a player successfully lands a hit on one of his guards or moves within 10 feet of the server console.
